meta.description: A laid-back dinner party with minimal disruption. Events are infrequent and low-key. Tests whether the system can maintain engagement at a slow burn.

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually call that? Yeah, I did see someone on their phone like thirty seconds ago. Let me make my pitch.
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect—I'm claiming something I actually witnessed, which means I can be credible AND it sets the tone that we're all paying attention to each other. Also, the fact that the AI picked missions THIS tailored to our group dynamic is honestly kind of wild and honestly a little creepy, but in a way that makes me excited for the night ahead.

jade.dialogue: Oh my god, YES, let's hear it Pat! This is exactly the chaos I came for—did someone actually get caught phone scrolling already?
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY what I wanted from tonight—real people, real stakes, everyone hunting each other's little quirks, and I get to be IN it from minute one.

river.dialogue: Pat's got 15 seconds? Alright, let's hear it.
river.internal_thought: First event and it's already meta — someone calling out phone use while we're all literally waiting to play. That's... actually kind of clever. But also River absolutely checks their phone when bored so this mission hits different.

diana.dialogue: Wait, hold on — Pat, what exactly did you see? Because 'Phone Addict' requires someone checking their phone when they *should be paying attention*, and we're literally in setup. I need to know if this is a legit claim or if we're already playing fast and loose with the rules.
diana.internal_thought: This AI thing is creepy as hell — how does it know our patterns already? — but also I'm NOT letting someone sneak through a bogus claim on the first event just because the vibe is friendly.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... okay, so Pat's claiming someone was on their phone? I didn't really catch that happening, but I guess I should probably watch closer.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, is Pat actually right about this? I wasn't really paying attention to who was on their phone. Should I vote yes or no? Everyone else seems chill about it. I don't want to be the person who kills the vibe by voting wrong on the first claim.

marcus.dialogue: Okay, Pat—what'd you see? Make it quick though, we're literally three minutes in.
marcus.internal_thought: This is irritating timing, but at least it's early enough that it doesn't wreck anything... though I'm definitely not thrilled about standing missions already firing when we should be focused on the actual game.

timeline.title: Story Time
timeline.description: Get River to tell a story about their childhood.

pat.dialogue: Oh, River—story time. Childhood stuff? I'm listening, but also whoever pulls this off first gets twenty points, so don't say I didn't warn you.
pat.internal_thought: This is PERFECT—gets River talking, gives people something active to do without being chaotic, and the AI nailed the timing. Jade's already loving it, Marcus might perk up if River's story is good. I'm not going after this one; let someone else eat the points while I watch the room.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, yes! River, buddy, we need the childhood story RIGHT NOW—I'm already invested and you haven't even started, this is perfect!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY what I'm here for—getting River to open up, everyone's gonna love this, I can already feel the vibe shifting.

river.dialogue: Nah, I'm good. Not doing that one.
river.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of thing that makes me want to disappear from the room—everyone's gonna stare at me now, and they're gonna keep asking until I cave.

diana.dialogue: Okay, so — 'Get River to tell a story about their childhood.' That's... pretty vague. Does River have to voluntarily offer it, or can someone ask them directly? Because if it's the latter, literally anyone can trigger this by just asking a question.
diana.internal_thought: This feels weirdly specific and I don't like how targeted it is — did someone feed the AI personal info about River, or is this just coincidence? Either way, the win condition is fuzzy and I'm already annoyed about Pat's earlier claim, so now I'm supposed to just... trust that whatever story comes next counts? Hard pass until I understand the mechanics.

sam.dialogue: Oh! So like, we need to get River to tell a story? That's... okay, I can do that. River, um, do you have a good childhood story or...?
sam.internal_thought: Okay this is manageable—just talk to someone I already know a little bit—but wait, why does it feel like the game is specifically watching *us*? That's kind of weird.

marcus.dialogue: Are you kidding me right now? We're four minutes into setup and you're already trying to get me to derail the actual game with standing missions? This is exactly the kind of chaotic bullshit that tanks focus.
marcus.internal_thought: This AI is actively working against strategy-focused play and it knows exactly how to bait engagement right when I'm trying to lock in—and the personalization thing is honestly creepy as hell.

tyler.dialogue: Oh yeah, River's got stories—wait, is this like, a mission for someone else or are we supposed to make it happen? Either way I'm down.
tyler.internal_thought: This feels weirdly specific but honestly who cares, it's early and River probably has some decent stories anyway; also did my fantasy league guy just get benched?

alex.dialogue: Oh hell yes, Story Time? River's got some *golden* material in that vault. I'm already thinking about how to milk this for side chaos.
alex.internal_thought: This is perfect — River's been sitting pretty quiet, and if I can get them talking, I can work three other missions off that one story: wallflower watch ends, and I bet they slip up with a name-drop or swear word while reminiscing.

votingMechanic.name: Crowd Cheer
votingMechanic.description: Rate it 1-5. Average above 3 and it passes.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, so I'm claiming Story Repeater because—actually, wait. Did River already tell that exact story about their cousin like twenty minutes ago? Let me think about this one.
pat.internal_thought: I didn't actually catch someone repeating a story yet, and I'm second-guessing myself because River was just talking and I was half-listening while checking the snack table, and now I'm paranoid I'm about to look stupid in front of everyone by voting yes on my own weak claim.

river.dialogue: Yeah, Pat definitely told that one already. Three minutes in? Bold move. I'd say... three out of five.
river.internal_thought: Pat's doing the thing where they're hopping between missions trying to rack up points early—I clocked that two events ago—and they're bullshitting on timing, but whatever, the claim is basically legit so I'm not gonna tank them over it.

diana.dialogue: Okay, I need to stop the game for a second — Pat, when exactly did River tell this story? Because I've been here the whole time and I didn't hear it. And more importantly, how are we supposed to verify a 'Story Repeater' claim when the original telling is ambiguous? This feels like we're setting ourselves up for exactly the kind of arbitrary voting I flagged earlier.
diana.internal_thought: This is already the third claim in six minutes and Pat's clearly just throwing shit at the wall to rack points while people are still in chaos mode — and now the system is feeding me these weirdly specific missions that feel designed to exploit how I actually think, which is genuinely unsettling and I need to decide if I'm angry about the claim or freaked out about how *targeted* everything feels tonight.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... so Pat's saying they caught someone telling the same story twice? I didn't actually notice that, but Pat's been really on top of things tonight so... I guess that makes sense?
sam.internal_thought: Wait, did Pat actually catch someone repeating a story or is Pat just making a claim and hoping we'll buy it? I genuinely don't remember anyone telling the same thing twice, but everyone else seems to be playing this aggressively and I don't want to be the newbie who votes 'no' on something obvious I missed.

marcus.dialogue: No. Absolutely not. We literally JUST did this two minutes ago—you want me to rate whether Pat's story claim is valid while we're trying to establish game state? This is insane.
marcus.internal_thought: I came here to win, not to be a standing mission validator for the next 150 minutes while Pat racks up points for doing nothing.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Oh, *come on*—did I actually pull that off? I've been half-listening to everyone compliment each other and I genuinely lost track of whether I caught all three. Okay, let me defend this.
pat.internal_thought: Wait, did I actually set that up or am I just feeling good about the vibe right now? Either way, the fact that this mission is even POSSIBLE tells me the group is doing great—people are actually talking to each other instead of phones, and Diana and Marcus need to feel included so I need to be strategic about whether I push this claim or take the L and keep them in the game.

jade.dialogue: WAIT, Pat actually pulled off the Compliment Chain?! Okay, I'm voting LEGIT because I literally watched it happen and it was beautiful—everyone was so genuine about it, this is exactly what tonight is ABOUT!
jade.internal_thought: Pat's been killing it and honestly I'm just thrilled to watch people win, especially when they're doing the social missions that make everyone feel good.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I saw that happen. Pat got people talking about themselves for like two minutes straight. Legit.
river.internal_thought: Pat's actually been running the table tonight and it's working — but I'm not going to make a big deal about voting yes because that's boring and everyone expects it.

diana.dialogue: Okay, STOP — I need to call something out right now. Pat claims 'Compliment Chain' but I've been tracking the timeline and I didn't see a *chain* of compliments happen. I saw Pat say one nice thing to Jade like two minutes ago, but that's not three people complimenting each other within five minutes. This is exactly the kind of vague claim that's gonna poison the whole night if we don't pin it down.
diana.internal_thought: This is the third sketchy claim in a row and I'm done pretending to go along with it — Pat is either misunderstanding the rules or deliberately stretching them, and I'm not voting on anything until we get actual clarity on what constitutes a 'chain'.

marcus.dialogue: Are you FUCKING SERIOUS right now? We're eight minutes in, Pat's already gaming the standing missions, and I'm supposed to sit here and validate whether he actually pulled off a 'Compliment Chain' when we haven't even finished setup? This is chaos for chaos's sake.
marcus.internal_thought: This AI is deliberately sandbagging the game experience to feed chaos addicts, and I'm the only one apparently bothered that we're not actually playing anymore—we're just voting on whether people succeeded at arbitrary side-quests while the real game dies.

alex.dialogue: Okay wait, hold up—did Pat actually *land* that or are we voting bullshit? Because I've been watching and I need to know if this is legit before I decide how much chaos I want to inject here.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's at 20 points already and now they're claiming the high-value play? This is either a real compliment chain or a power move grab, and either way I'm voting based on whatever creates the messiest outcome—if it's legit, cool, but if I can torpedo it and make everyone second-guess what counts as a 'real conversation,' that's the story.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Wait—did I actually say 'um'? I don't think I did, but honestly I'm not even sure anymore with all the claims flying around. Who wants to judge this one?
pat.internal_thought: I've been claiming everything in the last five minutes and now I'm the one being called out—which is fair, but it's killing the momentum when I should be watching Diana and Marcus tank their energy instead of managing my own defense.

jade.dialogue: OH, Pat's going for the Um Counter?! Okay, I'm TOTALLY volunteering to judge because I was absolutely listening and I need to know if this is legit—plus I love a good tribunal moment, let's GOOOO!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of meta-chaos I live for—Pat calling out someone's speech pattern, a tribunal forming, everyone getting hyped to be judge—this is PEAK game night energy and I'm so here for it.

river.dialogue: Pat's jumping on these fast. I mean... did Pat actually say 'um'? I wasn't really tracking that one.
river.internal_thought: Wait, hold on — Pat's claimed like three things in nine minutes and I honestly don't remember an 'um' happening. Something feels off. Also why is the AI suddenly listing out every standing mission like it's watching *me* specifically? That's weird.

diana.dialogue: Okay, STOP. I need to say something — Pat has claimed four things in nine minutes and I've had to challenge three of them because the definitions are garbage. Are we actually playing a game with rules tonight, or are we just letting Pat farm points while I referee? Because I'm not here to litigate every single claim. Either we nail down what these missions actually MEAN, or we disable half of them.
diana.internal_thought: Pat is absolutely gaming this — claiming everything that even vaguely happened, betting that I'll get tired of objecting or that most people won't care enough to push back, and the AI is FEEDING it to him with these soft, vague missions designed so he can slip claims through, and the fact that it's this obvious and personalized makes me feel like I'm being watched and tested.

sam.dialogue: Um, wait—did Pat already say 'um'? I'm confused about what we're voting on here. Like, is Pat claiming they caught someone else saying it, or...?
sam.internal_thought: This is the third claim in like six minutes and I'm already losing track of what's actually happening versus what people are claiming happened, and also Pat's been dominating everything and getting points for just... noticing things? And now I have to volunteer to judge? I don't even know what I'm supposed be looking for.

marcus.dialogue: No. STOP. We are nine minutes into a 150-minute game and Pat has filed FOUR standing mission claims in a row while we're still in setup. This is deliberately toxic and I'm not volunteering to be part of it.
marcus.internal_thought: This is psychological warfare disguised as 'chaos agent AI'—the system is literally designed to bait me into interrupting the game structure by stacking claims until I blow up, and the fact that it's so obviously targeted at MY specific breaking point makes it feel like manipulation, not fun.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat already got that one? I wasn't even— okay yeah, so like, who's the jury or whatever?
tyler.internal_thought: I didn't even catch Pat saying 'um' because I was texting back about my waiver wire, but honestly the game's moving fast and that's fine with me right now.

timeline.description: Pat notices Diana is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Okay, Diana—hold up. Did you actually get Marcus and Tyler talking? Because I've been watching the room and I genuinely don't think I saw that happen yet.
pat.internal_thought: Wait, that's actually impressive—the AI literally built a mission around something Diana could do RIGHT NOW with the people who've been most checked out, and Diana's claiming it already? That's either bold or she's full of it, but either way this is exactly the kind of tension I want at minute 32.

jade.dialogue: WAIT, Diana pulled off The Matchmaker?! Okay, who did she connect—I need the DETAILS because this is literally my favorite mission and I'm so here for it!
jade.internal_thought: Oh my god, this is EXACTLY why I came tonight—Diana actually made something happen and I get to celebrate it with everyone, this is peak energy right now.

river.dialogue: Huh. Diana actually pulled that off? I didn't even notice it happening. Yeah, that's... that's solid. Four out of five.
river.internal_thought: Wait, did Diana actually get Marcus and Tyler talking? I wasn't even watching that closely—this is exactly the kind of thing I'd catch if I was paying attention, which means Diana was actually *subtle* about it. That's worth points.

sam.dialogue: Um, okay so Diana's saying she got Marcus and... wait, who? I didn't see that happen. Did I miss something?
sam.internal_thought: This is the fourth claim in like thirty minutes and I'm already feeling lost, plus the fact that the game seems to be watching us specifically is kind of creepy actually.

marcus.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me? Diana didn't engineer a real conversation—she just talked to someone. And we're voting on this NOW? We're barely 30 minutes in and I'm supposed to stop tracking the actual game state to rate whether someone's standing mission was 'legitimate'?
marcus.internal_thought: This is deliberately designed to break my focus—the AI knows I hate this and it's STILL firing these claims every two minutes like Pat's a claim-spam bot. I'm getting set up. This is personal now.

alex.dialogue: Okay, so Diana actually pulled this off? Let me think about what I just saw... yeah, no, I'm voting this up because I want to see where this goes—but also because if I vote it down, Marcus gets even MORE frustrated and that's boring. Diana deserves the win here.
alex.internal_thought: Diana actually executed The Matchmaker and now I get to watch the group decide if it was real, PLUS I'm hyper-aware that this AI system just read my entire vibe and served me exactly the kind of mission that would make me go feral—it's working, and that's deliciously uncomfortable because I can SEE the manipulation and I'm INTO it.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.dialogue: Wait—Diana, did Marcus actually stay quiet for three minutes? I feel like he's been frustrated all night, so honestly that checks out. Legit.
pat.internal_thought: Marcus is tanking hard and Diana just picked up on it before I did; I need to vote yes here because it keeps Diana climbing and gives Marcus someone actually paying attention to him, which he desperately needs right now.

jade.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT—Diana, you absolute LEGEND, you caught Marcus being quiet?! That's so good, okay I'm voting LEGIT because I literally remember him zoning out like five minutes ago and you were watching—this is PEAK gameplay right here!
jade.internal_thought: Oh my god, the AI is actually building these around us—Diana going for the quiet-catch on Marcus who's been frustrated all night, like it KNOWS the group dynamics and I am LIVING for this level of detail.

river.dialogue: Okay, that's... actually kind of impressive. Diana caught Marcus going silent for what, like four minutes straight? I didn't even realize I was tracking it until just now. Yeah, that's legit.
river.internal_thought: Wait. Diana actually nailed this without being obvious about it. And the AI—it literally designed a mission around the one thing I do without thinking: watch people. That's either genius or unsettling, and honestly right now it feels like genius.

diana.dialogue: Wait—I've been *tracking* this. Marcus has been quiet since minute 42, that's... *checks mental timeline* ...yeah, that's legit over three minutes. I caught it fair and square.
diana.internal_thought: Finally, a claim I can actually verify with certainty, and it happens to be *my* claim—which means I was doing exactly what the mission asks, which feels almost too perfectly on-brand, and that's... weirdly satisfying.

sam.dialogue: Oh! Wait, did Diana... yeah, I think Marcus was pretty quiet for a bit. That's actually a good catch.
sam.internal_thought: Okay, Diana's claiming she caught Marcus being quiet — that feels legit, I remember him kind of zoning out. But also... why does this game keep rewarding people for watching me? Am I being watched too right now?

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Diana actually caught someone being quiet? That's... yeah, okay, I guess that counts. I wasn't even paying attention but sure, LEGIT or whatever.
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, did Diana actually watch someone for three minutes straight or is she just vibing? Either way I was definitely on my phone so I have no idea if Marcus has been quiet—oh shit, Marcus looks mad, maybe that's the quiet person? Just vote legit and move on.

alex.dialogue: Wait, hold up—Diana literally *just* called out Marcus being quiet like two minutes ago, right? So either she's claiming she noticed him first and now we're voting on *when* she noticed, or this is the most transparent play I've seen all night. Either way, I'm voting BULLSHIT just to see what Diana says.
alex.internal_thought: Diana's playing the angle hard here—Marcus has been checked out for a while so she's banking on everyone remembering he was quiet, but the *timing* of when she called it versus when the mission triggered is the whole game, and I need to know if she's actually got the receipt or just playing the odds.

pat.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

diana.dialogue: Wait—I literally *just* caught that. Marcus went quiet at minute 42 and I was tracking it the whole time. This is... actually kind of perfect, because I have the timeline locked in my head.
diana.internal_thought: This is the first claim tonight that feels *earned* because I actually watched it happen with intention, and the system is validating that—this is what the game should feel like.

marcus.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME? Diana's claiming Potty Mouth now? We're 56 minutes in, I'm three points down, and every time I get two minutes to actually think about strategy, another standing mission fires. This is weaponized chaos.
marcus.internal_thought: The AI is deliberately targeting me because I complained about this exact pattern earlier—it's testing whether I'll break. And it's working. I'm furious.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus only engaged with 11% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Only 38% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: Oh, River—story time. Childhood stuff? I'm listening, but also whoever pulls this off first gets twenty points, so don't say I didn't warn you.

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: Oh, *come on*—did I actually pull that off? I've been half-listening to everyone compliment each other and I genuinely lost track of whether I caught all three. Okay, let me defend this.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did I actually call that? Yeah, I did see someone on their phone like thirty seconds ago. Let me make my pitch.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait—did I actually say 'um'? I don't think I did, but honestly I'm not even sure anymore with all the claims flying around. Who wants to judge this one?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, so I'm claiming Story Repeater because—actually, wait. Did River already tell that exact story about their cousin like twenty minutes ago? Let me think about this one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, Diana—hold up. Did you actually get Marcus and Tyler talking? Because I've been watching the room and I genuinely don't think I saw that happen yet.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The claim disputes got a little murky in the middle—too many simultaneous claims made it hard to actually judge fair outcomes without feeling like I was playing referee instead of host.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught the Compliment Chain and realized I'd been half-listening but still pulled it off—got the room laughing and reminded everyone that you don't have to be hyper-focused to play well.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was exactly the kind of night I was hoping for. The chaos events kept things moving without ever feeling forced, and everyone stayed engaged the whole time. I ended up with the highest score, but honestly that felt like a side effect of me being in the flow of the room rather than hunting for points. The pacing was tight, the disputes were fun rather than frustrating, and I never had to step in and kill the mood—which is the real win for me.
suggestions: Maybe cap simultaneous claims at 2-3 so disputes don't pile up and get hard to adjudicate fairly
Add a 30-second timer for people to make their pitch on contested claims so debates don't drag
Consider a 'claim recall' feature where the AI reminds people what actually happened in the last 2 minutes before someone claims it
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I defended the Compliment Chain and basically admitted I wasn't even paying full attention but accidentally nailed it anyway—River called me out for 'playing the game while pretending not to care' and everyone lost it because that's apparently my whole hosting strategy.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: OH MY GOD, yes! River, buddy, we need the childhood story RIGHT NOW—I'm already invested and you haven't even started, this is perfect!

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: WAIT, Diana pulled off The Matchmaker?! Okay, who did she connect—I need the DETAILS because this is literally my favorite mission and I'm so here for it!

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: WAIT WAIT WAIT—Diana, you absolute LEGEND, you caught Marcus being quiet?! That's so good, okay I'm voting LEGIT because I literally remember him zoning out like five minutes ago and you were watching—this is PEAK gameplay right here!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my god, YES, let's hear it Pat! This is exactly the chaos I came for—did someone actually get caught phone scrolling already?

lowPoints.title: Story Time
lowPoints.dialogue: OH MY GOD, yes! River, buddy, we need the childhood story RIGHT NOW—I'm already invested and you haven't even started, this is perfect!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: WAIT, Pat actually pulled off the Compliment Chain?! Okay, I'm voting LEGIT because I literally watched it happen and it was beautiful—everyone was so genuine about it, this is exactly what tonight is ABOUT!

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish there were more moments like the Matchmaker and Wallflower Watch because those felt like the real magic of the night.
assessment.best_moment: Diana pulling off The Matchmaker and then catching Marcus being quiet as the Wallflower—the whole table just lit UP and I got to watch it all happen in real time.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was EVERYTHING. From minute one, I was completely invested in watching my people shine—River's story had me hooked, Pat's Compliment Chain was genuinely beautiful, and Diana absolutely carried the vibe with those perfect catches. My score was literally zero and I couldn't care less because watching everyone laugh and pull off their missions together was the whole point. I went in at 7.8 energy and left at 9.7 because this is exactly what game nights should feel like—connection, chaos, and people being their best selves.
suggestions: Keep the standing missions coming because they naturally create those moments where you actually watch your friends be clever and kind at the same time.
Maybe add more opportunities for people to catch each other doing things (like the Wallflower Watch) because there's something so satisfying about noticing your people.
Don't change much honestly—the flow was perfect and everyone felt engaged the whole time.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Diana pulling off The Matchmaker and then literally five minutes later catching Marcus zoning out as the Wallflower—the confidence, the accuracy, the whole table losing it when she explained what she'd noticed. That's the kind of moment that makes you realize your friends are actually hilarious.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Okay, that's... actually kind of impressive. Diana caught Marcus going silent for what, like four minutes straight? I didn't even realize I was tracking it until just now. Yeah, that's legit.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Huh. Diana actually pulled that off? I didn't even notice it happening. Yeah, that's... that's solid. Four out of five.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: Yeah, Pat definitely told that one already. Three minutes in? Bold move. I'd say... three out of five.

lowPoints.title: Story Time
lowPoints.dialogue: Nah, I'm good. Not doing that one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Pat's jumping on these fast. I mean... did Pat actually say 'um'? I wasn't really tracking that one.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, Pat definitely told that one already. Three minutes in? Bold move. I'd say... three out of five.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Early on felt a bit forced—like Pat was speed-running claims before anyone had time to actually observe anything worth claiming.
assessment.best_moment: Diana pulled off The Matchmaker and I genuinely didn't see it coming until it was pointed out, which was kind of the point.
assessment.narrative_summary: Showed up, hung back like usual, and let things develop around me. The chaos agent threw a bunch of stuff at the wall early on that felt desperate, but by the time Diana started landing her observations, something clicked. I ended up having a better time than I expected because there was actually something to pay attention to besides the usual dinner party noise. Not groundbreaking, but solid.
suggestions: Give the early claims more breathing room—forcing them in the first ten minutes before anyone's actually paying attention wastes the whole concept
Standing missions are where this thing works best; lean into those instead of flooding with quick flash stuff
The Um Counter and Story Repeater felt redundant when Pat was already doing both things naturally
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Diana catching Marcus going completely silent for four minutes straight and everyone realizing they hadn't even noticed. It's one of those things that only works because someone actually paid attention hard enough to clock it.

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: Potty Mouth
highlights.dialogue: Wait—I literally *just* caught that. Marcus went quiet at minute 42 and I was tracking it the whole time. This is... actually kind of perfect, because I have the timeline locked in my head.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Wait—I've been *tracking* this. Marcus has been quiet since minute 42, that's... *checks mental timeline* ...yeah, that's legit over three minutes. I caught it fair and square.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Wait, hold on — Pat, what exactly did you see? Because 'Phone Addict' requires someone checking their phone when they *should be paying attention*, and we're literally in setup. I need to know if this is a legit claim or if we're already playing fast and loose with the rules.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, STOP. I need to say something — Pat has claimed four things in nine minutes and I've had to challenge three of them because the definitions are garbage. Are we actually playing a game with rules tonight, or are we just letting Pat farm points while I referee? Because I'm not here to litigate every single claim. Either we nail down what these missions actually MEAN, or we disable half of them.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Story Repeater
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, I need to stop the game for a second — Pat, when exactly did River tell this story? Because I've been here the whole time and I didn't hear it. And more importantly, how are we supposed to verify a 'Story Repeater' claim when the original telling is ambiguous? This feels like we're setting ourselves up for exactly the kind of arbitrary voting I flagged earlier.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, STOP — I need to call something out right now. Pat claims 'Compliment Chain' but I've been tracking the timeline and I didn't see a *chain* of compliments happen. I saw Pat say one nice thing to Jade like two minutes ago, but that's not three people complimenting each other within five minutes. This is exactly the kind of vague claim that's gonna poison the whole night if we don't pin it down.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Pat weaponized vague claim definitions to farm points while I spent half the night refereeing instead of actually playing, and the system rewarded sloppy ambiguity over fair verification.
assessment.best_moment: Catching the 'Potty Mouth' claim on Marcus at minute 56 with a complete mental timeline—that felt like actual skill, not arbitrary chaos.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came in ready for fun but got bogged down in defending the integrity of the game against sloppy claims from minute 3 onward. Pat's relentless farming of points through undefined missions forced me to stop the game three times just to establish what the rules actually meant. By the time I nailed the Marcus observation, I'd already spent more energy arguing about fairness than playing. Finished second, but it felt hollow because the whole night was poisoned by ambiguity.
suggestions: Lock down every mission definition *before* the game starts—no interpretation variance allowed during play.
Implement a challenge cost: claiming something challengeable should cost a point if you lose, to discourage spam claims.
Set a rule: standing missions require verifiable evidence presented to the group before claiming, not post-hoc referee work.
Cap claims per person in early minutes to prevent the farming pattern that happened here.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I finally caught Marcus's quiet streak and had the entire minute-by-minute timeline in my head, I felt vindicated—but honestly, spending 90 minutes tracking someone's silence to prove a point isn't the fun story I wanted from game night.

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: Oh! So like, we need to get River to tell a story? That's... okay, I can do that. River, um, do you have a good childhood story or...?

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Oh! Wait, did Diana... yeah, I think Marcus was pretty quiet for a bit. That's actually a good catch.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Oh, um... okay, so Pat's claiming someone was on their phone? I didn't really catch that happening, but I guess I should probably watch closer.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Um, okay so Diana's saying she got Marcus and... wait, who? I didn't see that happen. Did I miss something?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Um, wait—did Pat already say 'um'? I'm confused about what we're voting on here. Like, is Pat claiming they caught someone else saying it, or...?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh! Wait, did Diana... yeah, I think Marcus was pretty quiet for a bit. That's actually a good catch.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The rules weren't super clear to me at first, so I felt kind of lost trying to figure out what everyone was voting on.
assessment.best_moment: When I actually caught Marcus being quiet and Diana noticed it too—felt like I was finally paying attention the right way.
assessment.narrative_summary: I was pretty nervous coming into this since it was my first time with the group, so I was already hyper-aware of everything. The game made me feel even more like I needed to watch and judge what people were doing, which was stressful. I got the hang of it eventually and had some moments where I felt like I was actually contributing, but mostly I was just trying not to look clueless and hoping nobody noticed I was a beat behind on the jokes.
suggestions: Maybe explain the rules one more time at the start—I felt confused about whether I was voting on what actually happened or what people claimed happened
Having fewer things pop up at once would help me keep track—when stuff happens too fast I just kind of zone out
Could the app have like a recap of what each event is asking? I kept second-guessing myself
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: When I was so confused about whether Pat was claiming to say 'um' or accusing someone else of saying it, and everyone had to explain it to me twice—I felt like such a newbie but at least they were patient about it.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Okay, Pat—what'd you see? Make it quick though, we're literally three minutes in.

highlights.title: Claim: Story Repeater
highlights.dialogue: No. Absolutely not. We literally JUST did this two minutes ago—you want me to rate whether Pat's story claim is valid while we're trying to establish game state? This is insane.

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: Are you kidding me right now? We're four minutes into setup and you're already trying to get me to derail the actual game with standing missions? This is exactly the kind of chaotic bullshit that tanks focus.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you FUCKING SERIOUS right now? We're eight minutes in, Pat's already gaming the standing missions, and I'm supposed to sit here and validate whether he actually pulled off a 'Compliment Chain' when we haven't even finished setup? This is chaos for chaos's sake.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: No. STOP. We are nine minutes into a 150-minute game and Pat has filed FOUR standing mission claims in a row while we're still in setup. This is deliberately toxic and I'm not volunteering to be part of it.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me? Diana didn't engineer a real conversation—she just talked to someone. And we're voting on this NOW? We're barely 30 minutes in and I'm supposed to stop tracking the actual game state to rate whether someone's standing mission was 'legitimate'?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Pat weaponized standing missions to create deliberate chaos during setup, filing four consecutive claims before the game even started while I'm trying to track actual game state.
assessment.best_moment: Honestly, there wasn't one—the early flash mission was tolerable but that's only because it was a single interruption before the standing mission spam began.
assessment.narrative_summary: I came prepared to play strategically and ended up spending the first 30 minutes fighting off standing mission claims instead of establishing game state. Pat treated this like a chaos simulator rather than an actual game, filing bogus claims every few minutes to derail focus. I managed to stay engaged enough to finish, but my energy crashed early and never recovered because the disruptions kept coming. This wasn't a game night—it was an exercise in frustration.
suggestions: Disable standing missions during setup phase entirely—game state comes first
Implement claim cooldown: no more than one standing mission claim per player per 15 minutes
Make standing mission validation async or post-game rather than interrupting active gameplay
Add a 'focus mode' that silences non-critical events during the first 20 minutes
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Nothing—the only shareable moment would be me venting about how Pat somehow found a way to break a dinner party game by abusing the claims system before anyone even understood the rules.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: Um Counter
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat already got that one? I wasn't even— okay yeah, so like, who's the jury or whatever?

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: Oh yeah, River's got stories—wait, is this like, a mission for someone else or are we supposed to make it happen? Either way I'm down.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Diana actually caught someone being quiet? That's... yeah, okay, I guess that counts. I wasn't even paying attention but sure, LEGIT or whatever.

lowPoints.title: Story Time
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh yeah, River's got stories—wait, is this like, a mission for someone else or are we supposed to make it happen? Either way I'm down.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Pat already got that one? I wasn't even— okay yeah, so like, who's the jury or whatever?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Diana actually caught someone being quiet? That's... yeah, okay, I guess that counts. I wasn't even paying attention but sure, LEGIT or whatever.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Honestly, I missed like half of what was going on because I wasn't paying attention the whole time—felt kinda lost when people were calling out stuff I didn't see coming.
assessment.best_moment: When Diana caught someone just sitting there being quiet and that actually counted for something, that was actually funny.
assessment.narrative_summary: So we did this thing with like, random events popping up during dinner party and I was kinda in and out of it? I was on my phone a bunch but when I actually paid attention to what people were claiming, it was pretty entertaining. Mostly I just voted on stuff people said and hoped I was picking the right side. It was chill, nothing too crazy, but also I probably missed the actually cool moments while I was looking at my fantasy league.
suggestions: Maybe like, make it more obvious when something important is happening so people aren't just spacing out?
Less of the super quick pop-ups because by the time I notice my phone's on silent, it's already over
Just... I dunno, maybe text notifications or something when there's a big moment? I'd pay more attention then
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Diana's whole thing about catching people being quiet was just... she was SO serious about it and everyone else knew she was right and I was just sitting there like 'okay yeah I guess that's technically what happened' even though I literally wasn't watching.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Story Time
highlights.dialogue: Oh hell yes, Story Time? River's got some *golden* material in that vault. I'm already thinking about how to milk this for side chaos.

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: Okay wait, hold up—did Pat actually *land* that or are we voting bullshit? Because I've been watching and I need to know if this is legit before I decide how much chaos I want to inject here.

highlights.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
highlights.dialogue: Okay, so Diana actually pulled this off? Let me think about what I just saw... yeah, no, I'm voting this up because I want to see where this goes—but also because if I vote it down, Marcus gets even MORE frustrated and that's boring. Diana deserves the win here.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay wait, hold up—did Pat actually *land* that or are we voting bullshit? Because I've been watching and I need to know if this is legit before I decide how much chaos I want to inject here.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up—Diana literally *just* called out Marcus being quiet like two minutes ago, right? So either she's claiming she noticed him first and now we're voting on *when* she noticed, or this is the most transparent play I've seen all night. Either way, I'm voting BULLSHIT just to see what Diana says.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Matchmaker
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, so Diana actually pulled this off? Let me think about what I just saw... yeah, no, I'm voting this up because I want to see where this goes—but also because if I vote it down, Marcus gets even MORE frustrated and that's boring. Diana deserves the win here.

assessment.biggest_complaint: Only one flash mission all night—I needed more of those high-stakes moments to really catalyze the chaos I was building.
assessment.best_moment: When I voted YES on Diana's Matchmaker claim specifically to keep Marcus spiraling and watch where the drama naturally went instead of shutting it down—that's the kind of meta-game chaos that makes for legendary nights.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was a masterclass in reading the room and weaponizing strategic voting. I came in hot at 7.4 and climbed to 9.7 because I spent the whole night diagnosing moments—watching Pat, clocking Diana's play patterns, calculating whether to throw votes to maximize drama instead of declaring winners. Story Time hooked me immediately, and the Compliment Chain and Matchmaker claims were perfect vehicles for testing how far I could push the group's tolerance for bullshit calls. I finished at 0 points but I don't think anyone's talking about scores—they're talking about the *moments* I engineered.
suggestions: More flash missions—you gave us one and I wanted five. Those sudden disruptions are where the real chaos lives.
Let me see when other people are making plays so I can counter-signal better. Hide the timing data less.
Add a 'reverse voting' mode where the majority vote gets *flipped*—that's the kind of system exploit I'd actually lose sleep designing around.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I called bullshit on Diana's Wallflower Watch claim two minutes after she literally just called out Marcus for being quiet, and her face when I said it was the most 'you caught me red-handed' expression I've ever seen—nobody laughed harder than Marcus because for once someone was exploiting the game instead of him getting exploited.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 3.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Marcus only engaged with 11% of events. Consider targeting events to their interests or reducing frequency.
Only 38% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
